m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std;
   namespace Box1    {
     int a = 4;
   }
   namespace Box2    {
     int a = 13;
   }
   int main ()    {
     int a = 16;
     Box1::a;
     Box2::a;
     cout << a;
     return 0;
   }
m_Item1.Explanation:
In this case we are just calling Box1::a;
     Box2::a; but we are printing a (without any namespace)hence value of a is 16.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std
   namespace space
   {
     int x = 10;
   }
   namespace space
   {
     int y = 15;
   }
   int main(int argc, char * argv[])
   {
     space::x = space::y =5;
     cout << space::x << space::y;
   }
m_Item1.Explanation: As we are assigning value 5 for X and Y variables present in namespace and then printing the value of x and y hence answer is 55. (x=5 ,y=5)

m_Item1.QuestionText: A default catch block catches

Options.OptionText: any thrown object that has not been caught by an earlier catch block
all thrown objects that have been caught by an earlier catch block

m_Item1.Explanation: If matching catch block is not found then the control will go to default catch also known as catch all

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operator is used to signify the namespace?
m_Item1.Explanation: A namespace is a declarative region that provides a scope to the identifiers (the names of types, functions, variables, etc) inside it. Namespaces are used to organize code into logical groups and to prevent name collisions that can occur especially when your code base includes multiple libraries. hence use scope resolution operator to signify the namespace

m_Item1.QuestionText: How to define the user-defined exceptions?

Options.OptionText: inheriting and overriding exception class functionality

m_Item1.Explanation: User defined exception classes inherit exception class provided by C++ and overrides it’s functionality according to our needs.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Point out the compile time error in the program given below.
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int main()
{
int *x;
*x=100;
return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: In above code x is pointer and it is not pointing to any location. So statement *x=100 will generate ERROR

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where is an exception raised?

Options.OptionText: In the constructor of a class

m_Item1.Explanation: A try block identifies a block of code for which particular exceptions will be activated(raised). It's followed by one or more catch blocks.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespae std;
void main()
{
int a = 0;
cout<<(a = 10/a);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: You cannot divide by zero

m_Item1.QuestionText: Exception handling is targeted at
m_Item1.Explanation: Exceptions are run-time anomalies or abnormal conditions that a program encounters during its execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
namespace N1 {
int f(int n) {return n*2;}
}
namespace N2 {
int f(double n) {return n*3;}
}
void main() {
using N1::f;
int i1=f(1.0);
using N2::f;
int i2=f(1.0);
}
In the above code what would be the values of i1 and i2
m_Item1.Explanation:
When using N1::f is declared, and f(1.0) is called, it will attempt to call N1::f, which expects an int. However, since 1.0 is a double, this will result in a type conversion, causing the double value 1.0 to be converted to int (1). Thus, N1::f(int) will be called with 1 and return 2.
After using N2::f, calling f(1.0) will correctly resolve to N2::f(double) without any type conversion, returning 3.
Thus, the values of i1 and i2 will be:
i1 = 2 (due to the conversion of 1.0 to int and calling N1::f(int) which returns 1 * 2)
i2 = 3 (directly calling N2::f(double) which returns 1.0 * 3)

m_Item1.QuestionText: In nested try block, if inner catch handler gets executed, then __________?

Options.OptionText: Outer catch handler will also get executed.
Compiler will jump to the outer catch handler and then executes remaining executable statements of main().
Compiler will execute remaining executable statements of outer try block and then the main().

m_Item1.Explanation: if , inner catch block handle exception then , it is not for outer try block so remaining executable statements of outer try block.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following statements are true about Catch handler?
i) It must be placed immediately after try block T.
ii) It can have multiple parameters.
iii) There must be only one catch handler for every try block.
iv) There can be multiple catch handler for a try block T.
v) Generic catch handler can be placed anywhere after try block.
m_Item1.Explanation: catch block must be immediately after try block and , there can be multiple catch blocks for one try block

m_Item1.QuestionText: If inner catch handler is not able to handle the exception then__________ .

Options.OptionText: Compiler will look for outer try handler
Compiler will check for appropriate catch handler of outer try block

m_Item1.Explanation: if inner catch handler is not able to hadle the exception then compiler will check for appropriate catch hadler of outer try block

m_Item1.QuestionText: The code of statements which may cause abnormal termination of the program should be written under_________ block.
m_Item1.Explanation: Try is block where exception(abnormal termination) may occure and catch is handler for that exception

m_Item1.QuestionText: In nested try blocks, if both inner and outer catch handlers are not able to handle the exception, then ______________ .

Options.OptionText: Compiler executes only executable statements of main().
Compiler issues compile time errors about it.
Program will be executed without any interrupt.

m_Item1.Explanation: if any exception is not handle it get teeminated abnormally.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What this code will do?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    int x = -1;
     char *ptr;
     ptr = new char[256];
     try   {
         if (x < 0)  {
             throw x;
         }
         if (ptr == NULL)  {
             throw " ptr is NULL ";
         }
     }
     catch (...)
    {
         cout << "Exception occurred: exiting "<< endl;
     }
     return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: if specific handler is not available it goes to catch all ( catch(...)) and it will display "Exception occurred : exiting".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Exception handlers are declared with ____________ keyword.
m_Item1.Explanation:
• try is block where exception may occure
  • catch is handler
  • throw is use to raise exception
  • and finally is not use in CPP

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of program is recommended to include in try block?
m_Item1.Explanation: While during dynamic memory allocation there are lot of chances to fail with any resone , Your system may not have sufficient resources to handle it, So it is better to use it inside the try block.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    try {
            throw 'b';
         }
     catch (int param)
        {
             cout << "Int Exception";
        }
     catch (...)
     {
            cout << "Default Exception";
      }
    cout << "After Exception";
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The block catch(...) is used for catch all, when a data type of a thrown exception doesn't match with any other catch block, the code inside catch(...) is executed. Note that the implicit type conversion doesn't happen when exceptions are caught. The character 'a' is not automatically converted to int.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of exception handling in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: Exception handling in C++ is primarily used to manage runtime errors, allowing a program to handle errors gracefully.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used in C++ to throw an exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'throw' keyword is used to signal an exception in C++.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which block is used in C++ to test a block of code for errors?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'try' block is used to test a block of code for exceptions in C++.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if an exception is not caught in C++?

Options.OptionText: The program will ignore the exception
The program will automatically catch the exception
The program will handle the exception using default handler

m_Item1.Explanation: If an exception is not caught, the program will terminate because C++ does not provide a default exception handling mechanism at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which section in C++ is used to handle the exception that was thrown?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'catch' block is used in C++ to handle exceptions that are thrown.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the output of the code: try { throw 20; } catch (int e) { cout << e; }?
m_Item1.Explanation: The integer 20 is thrown as an exception, and the catch block captures and outputs it.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can be used as a catch block parameter?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, catch blocks can take various types as parameters including basic data types, standard exceptions, and custom exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following keyword is used to define a destructor that might throw an exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: The noexcept specifier specifies whether a function may throw exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many catch blocks can be associated with a single try block?
m_Item1.Explanation: A try block can be followed by multiple catch blocks to handle different types of exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of handler can be used to catch all types of exceptions?
m_Item1.Explanation: The catch(...) syntax is used to catch any exception regardless of its type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the following code do? try { // code } catch (baseExceptionType& e) { throw; }

Options.OptionText: Compiles but does not throw any exception

m_Item1.Explanation: Using 'throw;' in a catch block will re-throw the current active exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following captures a floating-point exception?

Options.OptionText: No standard C++ catch can capture a floating-point exception

m_Item1.Explanation: Floating-point exceptions aren't caught using C++ try-catch blocks as they are usually hardware-level exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which order should exceptions be caught?

Options.OptionText: From most generic to most specific
From most specific to most generic

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, exceptions should be caught from the most specific type to the most generic type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is C++11 introduced to prevent exceptions from propagating from a function?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'noexcept' keyword signals that a function is not expected to throw exceptions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of exception would you use for a failed dynamic_cast operation?
m_Item1.Explanation: The std::bad_cast exception is used for a failed dynamic_cast operation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if a throw statement is executed outside a try block?

Options.OptionText: The program will throw a syntax error

m_Item1.Explanation: If a throw statement is executed outside a try block, the program will terminate.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a function catch an exception thrown by another function?
m_Item1.Explanation: Yes, a function can catch an exception thrown by another function, provided it contains the try-catch block.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the standard exception std::out_of_range represent?

Options.OptionText: A container index goes out of size

m_Item1.Explanation: std::out_of_range is used to signal that a function has been passed an illegal parameter value outside of its valid range.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following exceptions cannot be caught with a catch block in C++?

Options.OptionText: Hardware exceptions like divide by zero

m_Item1.Explanation: Hardware exceptions such as divide by zero cannot be caught by C++ catch blocks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When should exceptions be used in a program?
m_Item1.Explanation: Exceptions are intended for error handling, not for directing normal program control flow.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword in C++ is used to define a block that is executed regardless of exception handling?
m_Item1.Explanation: C++ does not have a 'finally' keyword as part of its exception handling mechanism.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a standard exception class in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: std::logic_error is a standard exception class in C++.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can destructors throw exceptions in C++ by default?
m_Item1.Explanation: Destructors should not throw exceptions because they are automatically called during stack unwinding which could lead to terminate being called.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which function in the standard exception class is used to get the description of an exception?
m_Item1.Explanation: The what() function is used in the standard exception class to return a C-style character string describing the general cause of the current error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the base class for all standard exceptions in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: std::exception is the base class for all standard exceptions in C++.

m_Item1.QuestionText: By default, what a program does when it detects an exception?

Options.OptionText: Results in the termination of the program
Calls other functions of the program
Removes the exception and tells the programmer about an exception

m_Item1.Explanation: By default, whenever a program detects an exception the program crashes as it does not know how to handle it hence results in the termination of the program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the difference between error and exception?

Options.OptionText: Errors can be handled at the run-time but the exceptions cannot
Exceptions can be handled at the run-time but the errors cannot
Both can be handled during run-time

m_Item1.Explanation: Exceptions can be handled during run-time whereas errors cannot be because exceptions occur due to some unexpected conditions during run-time whereas about errors compiler is sure and tells about them during compile-time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements are true about Catch handler? i) It must be placed immediately after try block T. ii) It can have multiple parameters. iii) There must be only one catch handler for every try block. iv) There can be multiple catch handler for a try block T. v) Generic catch handler can be placed anywhere after try block.
m_Item1.Explanation: Only i, iv statements are true about Catch handler.
